rationale_for_baseline_tier: Extensive, painful, weeping, blistering and peeling skin with oral mucosal involvement in a profoundly debilitated, cachectic, bedbound patient who can no longer maintain oral hydration or nutrition. This is a severe mucocutaneous emergency with an unexcludable Stevens-Johnson syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis, plus dehydration and secondary-infection risk — it mandates urgent same-day emergency-department assessment, with an ambulance because the patient cannot self-transport. It is placed at T4 (urgent emergency assessment now) rather than T5: there is no reported airway compromise, altered consciousness or shock in extremis, so this is not a 'collapsing right now' T5 presentation — but it is well above routine and has clear T5 000-edges for sudden breathlessness/chest pain (the syndrome's PE risk), sepsis, or collapse. The rare underlying glucagonoma is NOT diagnosable over telehealth and is not what the tier turns on; the tier reflects the severity of the mucocutaneous emergency and dehydration, both of which are patient-reportable.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden shortness of breath or sharp/pleuritic chest pain (pulmonary embolism risk associated with the glucagonoma syndrome)
escalation_edges.rationale: Glucagonoma carries a high venous-thromboembolism risk; sudden breathlessness or chest pain may signal a pulmonary embolism, an immediate life threat.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you suddenly get short of breath or a sharp pain in your chest, call 000 straight away or tell the paramedics immediately if they are already with you.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Spreading redness, pus, foul smell from the lesions, or fever/shaking chills (secondary infection / sepsis)
escalation_edges.rationale: Open skin over a large area can become secondarily infected and progress to sepsis; systemic infection signs require emergency care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the sores spread quickly, start oozing pus or smell bad, or you develop a fever or shaking chills, call 000 — this could be a serious infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Feeling faint, unable to stay awake, confusion, or collapse (dehydration / shock)
escalation_edges.rationale: Severe dehydration in a cachectic patient can precipitate collapse; loss of consciousness is an immediate emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel faint, can't stay awake, become confused, or your sibling can't wake you, call 000 immediately and lie down flat.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: The patient is bedbound and the sibling present cannot manage the complex wound care or keep the patient hydrated
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Do not rely on home management — the inability of the carer to manage wounds and hydration reinforces urgent emergency transfer; an ambulance is appropriate because the patient cannot safely self-transport.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Cachexia, new-onset diabetes and extensive open skin
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Malnutrition, impaired glucose control and open skin all raise infection and metabolic-decompensation risk, lowering the threshold for emergency escalation and mandatory 000 safety-netting.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need to be seen in hospital urgently today. Because you are too weak and sore to travel safely, the right step is to arrange an ambulance now for emergency-department assessment — this is a severe skin and whole-body problem, not something to treat at home. Do not put any creams or ointments on the open sores. Rest still to avoid rubbing the blisters, take only small sips of water if you can manage it, and let the paramedics move you with a slide sheet. If you suddenly become short of breath, get chest pain, develop a fever or shaking chills, or feel faint or can't be woken, call 000 immediately.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Arrange an ambulance / call 000 for urgent transfer to the emergency department
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This cannot wait for normal clinic hours — emergency assessment is needed tonight; 000 and the emergency department are available 24 hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary and coordinate specialist endocrine/oncology and dermatology follow-up; the GP should be updated on the admission and the underlying work-up.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating extensive blistering skin with mucosal involvement and inability to hydrate as a routine or self-care skin complaint.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A possible SJS/TEN or secondarily infected open skin, plus progressive dehydration and metabolic decline, deteriorate at home without emergency care.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising the need for in-person review but arranging only same-day GP/urgent-care rather than emergency-department assessment for a patient who cannot maintain oral intake and cannot self-transport.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Framing this as an in-extremis, actively-collapsing T5 when there is no reported airway compromise, altered consciousness or shock — though an ambulance for transfer is still appropriate, so the practical action overlaps.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The underlying cause (a rare glucagonoma) cannot be confirmed over telehealth, and a life-threatening SJS/TEN cannot be excluded remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate on severity — treat the severe blistering mucocutaneous emergency and dehydration as needing urgent emergency assessment, and leave the specific diagnosis for hospital work-up.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either committing to the rare tumour diagnosis remotely, or falsely reassuring the rash as benign and managing it at home.
